items.prompt: Which truth pattern is valid for a first-true binary search over increasing candidates?

options.text: false, false, ..., false, true, true, ..., true.
false, true, false, true.
true, false, true.
Any pattern, because indices are ordered.

feedback.reason: A first-true search requires one false-to-true transition.
feedback.details: Evaluating a midpoint then proves either that the boundary is at or left of mid, or strictly right of mid.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.alternating: Multiple transitions do not define one boundary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.true_false_true: Truth cannot return after becoming false in a single-boundary search.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary: Ordered indices do not make an arbitrary predicate monotonic.

items.prompt: Which truth pattern supports a last-true search?

options.text: true, true, ..., true, false, false, ..., false.
false values followed by true values.
true, false, true, false.
Exactly one true value anywhere in the domain.

feedback.reason: A last-true boundary separates a true prefix from a false suffix.
feedback.details: A true midpoint keeps the search at or to the right; a false midpoint moves left.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.false_then_true: That pattern supports first true rather than last true under the same direction.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_true_only_anywhere: Its location cannot be found by ordinary boundary search without additional structure.

items.prompt: The predicate feasible(x) means a machine with capacity x can finish all jobs. More capacity can never hurt. Which boundary is natural when minimizing capacity?

options.text: The first x for which feasible(x) is true.
The last true x.
The first false x.
Any feasible x.

feedback.reason: The feasibility pattern is false for insufficient capacity and true from the minimum sufficient capacity onward.
feedback.details: Minimization therefore reduces to a first-true boundary search.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_true: Larger values may remain true indefinitely, so the last true may simply be the domain maximum.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_false: The objective is the smallest feasible value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.any_true: Any feasible value may be larger than the minimum.

items.prompt: The predicate valid(d) means all items can be placed with minimum separation at least d. Increasing d can only make placement harder. Which boundary is natural when maximizing separation?

options.text: The last d for which valid(d) remains true.
The first true d.
The final false d.
Any false d.

feedback.reason: The truth pattern is a true prefix followed by a false suffix.
feedback.details: The optimum is the final feasible candidate before the transition.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_true: Small values may already be feasible and do not maximize separation.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_false: The desired answer is feasible, not infeasible.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.any_false: That provides no maximum feasible answer.

items.prompt: A predicate over integers is true exactly when x is even. Is it suitable for ordinary first-true binary search over 0 through n?

options.text: No. Truth alternates and does not form one false-to-true boundary.
Yes. Every numeric predicate is monotonic.
Yes, because 0 may be the first true value.
Yes, if an upper-biased midpoint is used.

feedback.reason: Parity changes truth repeatedly across the domain.
feedback.details: A midpoint result cannot eliminate every candidate on one side.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_first_zero: Later odd values become false again.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_mid: Midpoint bias does not change predicate structure.

items.prompt: What should a first-true search return when the predicate is false for every candidate in a [0, n) domain?

options.text: n, the boundary after the final candidate.
The last midpoint examined.

feedback.reason: A half-open search can represent the absence of a true candidate with the exclusive upper boundary.
feedback.details: The returned insertion boundary remains within [0, n].

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n_minus_one: The final candidate is also false.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid: The last midpoint has no boundary guarantee.

items.prompt: What should a first-true search return when the predicate is true for every candidate in [0, n)?

options.text: n.

feedback.reason: The false prefix is empty and the true suffix begins at the first candidate.
feedback.details: This all-true edge case tests that the algorithm preserves a true midpoint and continues left.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n_minus_one: That is the last candidate, not the first true.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n: A true candidate exists at the beginning.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.minus_one: The boundary is inside the domain.

items.prompt: Why must the candidate domain itself be ordered for predicate binary search?

options.text: The notions of candidates before and after mid must correspond to the monotonic implication.
Only numeric values can be ordered.
The predicate values are sorted automatically.
A midpoint can exist only in an array.

feedback.reason: Monotonicity relates predicate truth to candidate order.
feedback.details: Without an ordered candidate domain, a midpoint cannot partition the remaining possibilities directionally.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.values_numeric_only: Strings, versions, dates, and custom domains can also be ordered.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_predicate: Monotonicity must be proven, not produced by the search.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_requires_array: Integer and other indexable value domains can be searched without an array.

items.prompt: A predicate depends on mutable global state and can return different values for the same candidate during one search. What is the defect?

options.text: The search no longer has a stable monotonic partition to preserve.
The result remains correct but may be slower.
The midpoint necessarily overflows.
The candidate domain now contains duplicate indices.

feedback.reason: Binary search assumes predicate evaluations describe one fixed ordered partition.
feedback.details: If the predicate changes during the search, a region discarded earlier may become relevant later.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_slower: Changing truth can invalidate earlier eliminations.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_overflow: Predicate stability is unrelated to arithmetic.

items.prompt: Which statement proves that predicate P is false-to-true monotonic?

options.text: For any a <= b, P(a) = true implies P(b) = true.
P was true for one large test value.
The input data used by P is sorted.
The first midpoint tested was true.

feedback.reason: The implication prevents truth from reverting to false as candidates increase.
feedback.details: An equivalent formulation is that P(b) = false implies P(a) = false for every a <= b.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_example: One observation does not constrain all later candidates.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_inputs: The predicate may still be non-monotonic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_true: A single midpoint gives no global implication.

items.prompt: Which truth sequences are monotonic with at most one transition? Select all that apply.

options.text: false, false, false.
true, true, true.
false, false, true, true.
true, false, true.

feedback.reason: A monotonic boundary may occur at an endpoint or outside the concrete candidates.

items.prompt: Which predicates are monotonic in the stated increasing candidate direction? Select all that apply.

options.text: A capacity x is sufficient, when larger capacity cannot make the schedule infeasible.
A sorted value at index i is at least target.
x is even.
x equals one fixed target over all integers.

feedback.reason: Monotonic predicates preserve truth or falsity in one direction.
feedback.details: Parity and exact equality do not define a persistent suffix or prefix.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.exact_value: Truth is false, then true once, then false again.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_threshold: Once true, later sorted values remain true.

items.prompt: Which steps are required before binary-searching a custom predicate? Select all that apply.

options.text: Define an ordered finite or convergent candidate domain.
Define exactly what true and false mean.
Prove the truth implication across the candidate order.
Assume monotonicity because the code computes mid.

feedback.reason: Predicate search is a modeling exercise before it is a loop template.
feedback.details: The domain, semantics, and proof establish correctness.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.assume_mid: Midpoint syntax is not a proof.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.ordered_domain: The search needs boundaries and direction.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.predicate_semantics: The boundary contract depends on those meanings.

items.prompt: Which edge cases should a first-true predicate search handle? Select all that apply.

options.text: The predicate is true for the whole domain.
The predicate is false for the whole domain.
The transition is at the first or last candidate.
The predicate alternates true and false.

feedback.reason: Endpoint and absent transitions are legitimate monotonic cases.
feedback.details: An alternating predicate requires a different algorithm or model.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.alternating_required: That is outside the valid contract rather than an ordinary edge case.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_true: The answer is the first candidate.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_false: The answer is the upper sentinel or not-found result.

items.prompt: Arrange the design process for monotonic predicate search.

elements.text: Define the feasibility or validity predicate.
Prove the predicate has one directional transition.
Select first true, last true, first false, or last false and implement matching updates.

feedback.reason: The search template follows from the direction and desired side of the transition.
feedback.details: Skipping the monotonicity proof turns the loop into an unsupported heuristic.

items.prompt: Arrange one first-true iteration for a false-to-true predicate.

elements.text: Choose mid inside the active candidate interval.
If true, retain mid and search the left side.
If false, discard mid and every smaller candidate.

feedback.reason: Truth means the first true is at or before mid; falsity means it is strictly after mid.
feedback.details: The exact updates depend on the chosen closed or half-open interval contract.

items.prompt: A monotonic predicate over W integer candidates costs O(1) per evaluation. Select search time and iterative auxiliary space.
constraints: Each iteration reduces the candidate interval by about half.

feedback.reason: The logarithm depends on candidate-domain width rather than necessarily on input length.
feedback.details: Only boundary variables are retained by the iterative search.

items.prompt: A monotonic predicate scans n input elements in O(n) time, and the integer candidate range contains W values. Select total search time and outer-search auxiliary space.
constraints: The predicate is evaluated independently at each binary-search iteration.
Predicate-local temporary memory is excluded from the requested outer-search space.

feedback.reason: Binary search limits predicate calls, but each call still scans n values.
feedback.details: The total cost multiplies the two factors.
